keyEvents.impact: Federal state built on English-French compromise; accommodation as a governing instinct
Coming-of-age moment; national identity forged through contribution to allied wars
Security permanently tied to collective defense alongside the US and Europe

keyEvents.event: Pearson invents UN peacekeeping during Suez
keyEvents.impact: Middle-power identity; multilateralism and honest-broker diplomacy as national brand

keyEvents.event: Patriation of the Constitution and Charter of Rights
keyEvents.impact: Full sovereignty from Britain; rights-based liberal political culture

keyEvents.event: Quebec independence referendum fails by one percent
keyEvents.impact: Near-breakup of the country; permanent sensitivity to Quebec's status
Economy irrevocably integrated with the United States; trade dependence shapes all foreign policy

history.narrative: A wealthy middle power whose security and prosperity are inseparable from the United States, expressed through NORAD, NATO, and the world's largest bilateral trade relationship. It projects influence through multilateralism, peacekeeping tradition, and coalition contributions rather than independent hard power, while a warming Arctic turns its vast northern frontier into a growing strategic concern.

redLines.description: Armed attack on the United States or any NATO member
Violation of Canadian Arctic sovereignty or militarization of the Northwest Passage
Severing of North American trade and supply-chain integration

internalDivisions.name: Western alienation over energy and federal policy

goals.description: Maintain the US alliance and modernize NORAD continental defense
Assert Arctic sovereignty as melting ice opens northern waters to rivals
Protect trade access to the US market while diversifying toward Asia and Europe
Champion multilateral institutions, peacekeeping, and the rules-based order
